elements.sent-2411-001: A forced solution $v$ of the Navier-Stokes equation in any open domain with no slip boundary condition is constructed.
elements.sent-2411-002: The scaling factor of the forcing term is the critical order $-2$.
elements.sent-2411-003: The velocity, which is smooth until its final blow up moment, is in the energy space through out.
elements.sent-2411-004: Since most physical forces from a point source in nature are regarded as order $-2$, such as Coulomb force, Yukawa force, this result indicates possible singularity formation under these kind of forces.
elements.sent-2411-005: The result also holds for some log subcritical forces or some forces in the standard critical space $L^\infty_t L^{3/2 _x$.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The Navier-Stokes equations (NS) modeling the motion of viscous incompressible fluids in a domain $D \subset \bR^3$ are \be \lab{nse \mu \Delta v - v \nabla v - \nabla P -\partial_t v =F, \quad div \, v=0, \quad \text{in \quad D \times (0, \infty), \quad v(\cdot, 0)=v_0(\cdot) \ee The unknowns are the velocity $v$ and the pressure $P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Suitable boundary conditions are imposed if $\partial D \neq \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Due to Leray \cite{Le2 , if $D=\bR^3$, $v_0 \in L^2(\bR^3)$, $F=0$, the Cauchy problem has a solution in the energy space $(v, \nabla v) \in (L^\infty_t L^2_x, L^2_{x t )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, in general we do not know if such solutions stay bounded or regular for all $t>0$ given regular initial values.
elements.sent-2411-010: The uniqueness of such solutions is also unknown so far.
elements.sent-2411-011: Solutions beyond the energy space or super critical forcing terms have been investigated before.
elements.sent-2411-012: Recall that for the forcing term, the space $L^\infty_t L^{3/2 _x$ is critical.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Let us recall that a forcing term in local $L^q_tL^p_x$ space is super critical, critical and sub critical if $\frac{3 {p + \frac{2 {q >, =, < 2$ respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In general, under the standard scaling, a forcing term is super critical, critical and sub critical if its scaling order is less than, equal to or greater to $-2$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-015: For a forcing term in the supercritical space $L^1_t L^2_x$, in the paper \cite{ABC22 , non-uniqueness of \eqref{nse in the energy space is established.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In the homogeneous case $F=0$, non-uniqueness was proven for some solutions in the space $L^\infty_t L^2_x$ in \cite{BV .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recently, an instantaneous finite time blow up example was constructed in \cite{LPYZZZ for a special cusp domain.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The solution is in the energy space and the forcing term (Ampere force) is actually subcritical.
elements.sent-2411-019: These results indicate that if pertinent function spaces or the domain are sufficiently singular, then something singular can happen to the solutions.
elements.sent-2411-020: In a related development, forced blow up solutions for some hypo-dissipative Navier Stokes equations was studied in \cite{CMZ .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In a previous note \cite{Zq23 , a forced solution $v$ of the axially symmetric Navier-Stokes equation in a finite cylinder $D$ with suitable boundary condition is constructed.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The divergence free, rotational forcing term has an order of scaling which is a little worse than the critical order $-2$.
elements.sent-2411-024: One can argue that this is not too surprising since for any smooth, divergence free vector field $v$ that blows up in a given final time, the following trivial equation holds before blow up: \begin{equation}\Delta v -\partial_t v -v \nabla v = F \equiv \Delta v -\partial_t v -v \nabla v\end{equation} Then this vector field $v$ is a solution to a forced Navier-Stokes equation.

elements.sent-2411-113: For example, making the change of variables $z_1=x_2$, $z_2=x_1$ and $z_3=x_3$, we have \begin{equation}\al (\p^2_2+\p^2_3) (-\Delta)^{-1} h_1(x, t)=\frac{1}{4\pi}(\p^2_{x_2}+\p^2_{x_3}) \int \frac{ h_1(y, t)}{\sqrt{|x_1-y_1|^2+|x_2-y_2|^2+|x_3-y_3|^2}} dy =\frac{1}{4\pi}(\p^2_{z_1}+\p^2_{z_3}) \int \frac{ h_1(y_1, y_2, y_3, t)}{\sqrt{|z_2-y_1|^2+|z_1-y_2|^2+|z_3-y_3|^2}} dy =\frac{1}{4\pi}(\p^2_{z_1}+\p^2_{z_3}) \int \frac{ h_1(y_2, y_1, y_3, t)}{\sqrt{|z_2-y_2|^2+|z_1-y_1|^2+|z_3-y_3|^2}} dy, \quad (y_2 \to y_1, y_1 \to y_2), =\frac{1}{4\pi}(\p^2_{z_1}+\p^2_{z_3}) \int \frac{ h_1(y_1, y_2, y_3, t)}{\sqrt{|z_1-y_1|^2+|z_2-y_2|^2+|z_3-y_3|^2}} dy, \quad (h_1 \,  is radial), =\frac{1}{4\pi}(\p^2_{x_1}+\p^2_{x_3}) \int \frac{ h_1(y, t)}{\sqrt{|x_1-y_1|^2+|x_2-y_2|^2+|x_3-y_3|^2}} dy. \quad (z_i \to x_i, i=1, 2, 3.) \eal\end{equation}Notice that due to the change of variables, the above argument does not infer that the three functions are equal pointwise.
elements.sent-2411-114: What being shown is that \begin{equation}\Vert (\p^2_2+\p^2_3) (-\Delta)^{-1} h_1 \Vert_\infty = \Vert (\p^2_1+\p^2_3) (-\Delta)^{-1} h_1 \Vert_\infty= \Vert (\p^2_1+\p^2_2) (-\Delta)^{-1} h_1 \Vert_\infty.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-158: The result can also be considered as a step in Scheffer's plan proposed in \cite{Sc p49.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The problem with this almost effortless process is that the forcing term $F$ is super critical or nonphysical, as pointed out long time ago on p50 of \cite{Sc .}
elements.sent-2411-161: Scheffer constructed a blow up solution of the forced Navier Stokes equations in the energy space with a very singular force $F$.
elements.sent-2411-162: The main novelty is that $F \cdot v \ge 0$ in our sign convention so the force might seem to slow down the growth of the velocity.
elements.sent-2411-163: Scheffer's result is a step in his plan to construct singular solutions of the Navier-Stokes equation in the energy space, but he himself characterizes the force as "a bizarre function that would not come up in real life situations", c.f.. line 5, p.
elements.sent-2411-164: In this paper, we improve the results in \cite{Zq23 and, to some degree, \cite{Sc by showing some critical forces or even log sub-critical forces can also generate finite time singularity, although we should add that the sign of $F \cdot v$ is not controlled.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: The result can also be considered as a step in Scheffer's plan.
